institution_connections.notes: One of the Seven Pillars of Farmington church founding 1652

children_documented.name: John Hart
children_documented.notes: Survived the 1666 Indian fire; ancestor of the three Hart brothers who married three Hooker sisters

children_documented.name: Capt. Thomas Hart
children_documented.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726, m. Ruth Hawkins. Civic co-equal of John Hooker (H00030). His son Deacon Thomas Hart (X00043) married into the Hooker family — father of Mercy Hart who married John Hooker No. 2 (H00098). Lineage confirmed: Stephen Hart → Capt. Thomas → Deacon Thomas → Mercy → compiler's ancestor.

parents.notes: Parents unknown. English origin, likely Braintree, Essex.

narrative_blocks.header: One of Farmington's Seven Pillars and first deacon
narrative_blocks.body: When the Farmington church was organized in November 1652, he stepped forward as one of its seven founding pillars and became the town's first deacon. He had already served as Thomas Hooker's deacon at both Cambridge and Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: Tradition: Hartford was named for him
narrative_blocks.body: Tradition holds that when Hooker's company crossed the Connecticut River in 1636, Hart found a ford where the water was low — making 'Hart's Ford' the seed of the city's name. His descendant Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps put it in verse around 1871.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandchildren married three Hooker daughters
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson Capt. John Hart had three sons who each married a daughter of John Hooker — Thomas Hooker's grandson. Three Harts and three Hookers married in a single generation, knitting the families together.

narrative_blocks.header: Indians burned his son's family in 1666
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1666 a fire set by Indians destroyed his son John's house at Farmington, killing the entire family except John Jr. — who happened to be tending stock at Avon that night. The blaze also destroyed Farmington's town records.

narrative_blocks.header: Original 1639 Hartford proprietor and Farmington miller
narrative_blocks.body: He was an original proprietor of Hartford in 1639 and one of 84 proprietors of Farmington in 1672. His Farmington house-lot of 15 acres — the town's largest — was granted on condition he run the mill.

cross_connections.display_label: was Hart's minister at Newtowne; led him to Hartford
Both helped govern Farmington for decades.
Four of his grandchildren married four Standley children.

parents.notes: Son of John Hart (X01887) who perished in the 1666 fire

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. FindAGrave Memorial ID: 77180356.

narrative_blocks.header: Away tending stock the night his family died
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1666 a fire set to the Hart house in Farmington killed everyone inside; John, then about eleven, survived only because he had been sent that night to Nod to tend the family's stock. He grew up the sole survivor of his household.

narrative_blocks.header: Three grandsons wed three of Hooker’s granddaughters
narrative_blocks.body: John's grandfather, Deacon Stephen Hart, had served Rev. Thomas Hooker's own church in Hartford. A generation later, three of John's grandsons — the brothers Matthew, Samuel, and Nathaniel Hart — married three granddaughters of Thomas Hooker, knotting the two families together within a few years.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandfather’s will singled the orphan out
narrative_blocks.body: When Deacon Stephen Hart wrote his will in 1682, he named the fire's lone survivor directly as his eldest son's son. The boy who had lost everyone kept his place in the family record and a small legacy.

notable.notable_blurb: Farmington militia captain, General Court speaker

cross_connections.display_label: shared with him the leadership of early Farmington

bio.title: Captain
bio.bio_blurb: Farmington militia captain; General Court Speaker

narrative_blocks.header: One of Farmington’s two prominent men
narrative_blocks.body: Contemporaries paired him with John Hooker as “the two prominent men of Farmington” — ensign to captain of the trainband, Speaker of the General Court, and seventeen years a Hartford County justice. His son Deacon Thomas married into the Hooker family.

open_questions.question: Deacon Thomas Hart vs Deacon Ebenezer Hart — identity of Mercy Hart's father
open_questions.notes: EH entry 98 gives Mercy Hart's father as 'Deacon Thomas Hart'. FVR (Barbour Collection) gives 'John, Jr., m. Mary HEART, d. Dea. Ebenezer' — suggesting Deacon Ebenezer Hart. Could be two different Deacons, or EH/FVR error on first name. Andrews' Stephen Hart and his Descendants should resolve this.
open_questions.action: Consult Andrews' Stephen Hart genealogy; check Kensington church deacon records

sources.full: Alfred Andrews, Genealogical History of Deacon Stephen Hart and his Descendants (New Britain, CT), pp. 364-5

burial.plot_notes: Christian Lane Cemetery, Kensington, Hartford County, Connecticut

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Thomas Hart of Farmington — son of Stephen Hart (X00034), deacon of Rev. Thomas Hooker's church. Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726, married Ruth Hawkins.

political_offices.title: Representative
political_offices.notes: Represented town of Farmington often in the General Court. EH source: 'man of great dignity and influence'

church_roles.significance: First of four consecutive generations of deacons in this family: Thomas³ → Elijah⁴ → Elijah⁵ → Elijah⁶
church_roles.ordination_charge: I charge thee before God and the Lord Jesus Christ... that thou be faithful to the trust that is committed unto thee; thou art made a steward of the external good things of this Church.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Capt. Thomas Hart
narrative_blocks.body: Son of Capt. Thomas Hart — grandson of Stephen Hart, deacon of Rev. Thomas Hooker's original church

narrative_blocks.header: Deacon of Kensington church from 1718/9
narrative_blocks.body: Deacon of Kensington church from 1718/9 — first of four generations of deacons in the Hart family

narrative_blocks.header: Meeting house of Kensington built on his home
narrative_blocks.body: Meeting house of Kensington built on his home lot by order of the Connecticut Legislature, May session 1732

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace and town representative
narrative_blocks.body: Justice of the Peace and town representative to General Court

narrative_blocks.header: Among original ten members of Kensington church
narrative_blocks.body: Among original ten members of Kensington church at its founding December 10, 1712

narrative_blocks.header: His father Capt. Thomas Hart and John Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: His father Capt. Thomas Hart and John Hooker were described as 'the two prominent men of the town'

parents.mother_research_notes: Ruth Hawkins (daughter of Anthony Hawkins of Farmington)

church_roles.notes: Bespoken as tutor on the day of his graduation. Preceded by Daniel Hooker (first tutor, 1702), who was a son of Rev. Samuel Hooker of Farmington (H00007) and brother-in-law of Rev. James Pierpont, Yale's founding patron.

Granted 25 acres by town of Guilford as settlement. "One of the first Eminence of preachers in his Day" (Rev. Thomas Ruggles Jr.). "A gentleman of eminent piety" (Rev. John Devotion). About 80 members added to church during his pastorate. In 1722, signed Rector Cutler's petition expressing doubt of Presbyterian ordination — later resolved his doubts and remained in place. President Stiles: "Mr. Hart is said to have been a man of the greatest ingenuity and learning of all the seven."

narrative_blocks.header: Yale's first B.A. graduate — alone in 1703
narrative_blocks.body: He was the first student in Yale College to be advanced to a Bachelor's Degree. He graduated alone at Saybrook on September 15, 1703 — his diploma signed by Rector Abraham Pierson, Moses Noyes, Thomas Buckingham, and Noadiah Russell.

narrative_blocks.header: Second Yale tutor — succeeded Daniel Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: He was appointed Yale's second tutor on the very day of his graduation, succeeding his Farmington townsman Daniel Hooker. He served from 1703 to 1705, with charge of the two lower classes.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordained East Guilford's first minister, 1707
narrative_blocks.body: In April 1707 he accepted the call to East Guilford (now Madison), and on November 25, 1707 he was ordained over the new thirteen-member church. Five ministers including James Pierpont and Thomas Buckingham officiated.

narrative_blocks.header: Signed the 1722 Yale Episcopal declaration
narrative_blocks.body: In September 1722 he joined Rector Cutler and five others in signing the famous declaration before Yale trustees questioning the validity of Presbyterian ordination. After conference at Yale Library on October 16, his doubts were resolved and he remained Congregational.

narrative_blocks.header: Greatest ingenuity and learning of all seven
narrative_blocks.body: President Ezra Stiles called him a man of 'the greatest ingenuity and learning' among the seven who signed the 1722 declaration. His estate at death included £35 in books — a substantial scholarly library.

cross_connections.display_label: Succeeded him as Yale's second tutor in 1703

education.notes: FIRST student at Yale to receive a Bachelor's degree — graduated alone at Saybrook, September 15, 1703. His original diploma is preserved in Yale Library. The 1702 class received M.A. degrees only (honorary, for Harvard graduates); Hart's 1703 B.A. was Yale's first bachelor's degree.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Yale's first graduate; second tutor of the college

INST005.name: First Church of Christ, Farmington
INST005.notes: Roger Newton first pastor 1645-1657; Samuel Hooker second pastor 1661-1697. Seven Pillars at founding: Rev. Roger Newton, Stephen Hart, John Bronson, John Cole, Thomas Thompson, Thomas Judd, Robert Porter.
